title: Chapter 9: Ten Million and Counting
content: The champagne tastes like battery acid with a better publicist.

I'm holding a flute of it because that's what you do at these things — hold, sip, pose, repeat. The fizz hits my tongue and dies. Everything dies in my mouth lately. But I smile, because the ring light catches my face at this angle and that's the angle @MephistoMCN branded as my "signature look," and the influencer paparazzi are three deep along the railing, and I am the guest of honor at a party I didn't plan for a milestone I didn't choose.

The rooftop is Chelsea-level obscene. Mephisto MCN rented the whole building — I don't know what that costs and I don't want to — and they've decked it out like a tech startup's fever dream. White furniture that no one sits on. Geometric ice sculptures melting into themselves. A champagne tower that's somehow also a waterfall, glasses stacked six feet high with Dom Pérignon cascading down the pyramid in a continuous loop, recycled by some pump system I'm not supposed to notice. The bass from the DJ booth thuds through my sternum like a second heartbeat. Or a first one. Mine's been questionable.
And the screen.

There's a giant LED screen mounted above the skyline, right where the moon should be, and it's counting. Nine million, eight hundred and forty-two thousand, three hundred and sixteen. Nine million, eight hundred and forty-two thousand, three hundred and seventeen. The numbers tick up in real time, fed by whatever API Mephisto's platform uses to track my growing audience. Every new follower adds another digit to the sky. Every follow is a small subscription to the ongoing disassembly of Chloe Hart.

I'm wearing what the algorithm picked. I know that because my stylist — yes, I have a stylist now — showed me the data. This dress, this shade of burgundy, this hemline, these shoes. Sixty-three percent higher engagement in test markets. The algorithm knows me better than I do. The algorithm knows me better than I do because the algorithm is eating me.

I pose with someone. I don't know their name. They have three million followers and a jawline that could cut glass and they're telling me I'm "killing it" and I say "thank you" and I mean nothing. The nothing is new. Or maybe it's not new. Maybe I just notice it now because there's less of everything else.

The confetti hasn't fallen yet. It will. They're saving it for the big moment. Ten million. There are cases of the stuff stacked behind the DJ booth. I saw them when I walked in. Metallic, color-coordinated, biodegradable. Even our celebrations are eco-conscious.
A photographer asks me to tilt my chin. I tilt my chin.
Mephisto appears at 9,912,004.

He steps out from behind the champagne waterfall like it's a magic trick. Like he was always there, just waiting for the right moment of visual distraction. The crowd parts — not because they know who he is, but because he has that energy. That gravitational pull. People move without knowing why.

He's wearing a suit that costs more than my old apartment's annual rent. Charcoal, no tie, collar open. His hair is silver at the temples in a way that looks deliberate, like he chose to age just enough to be distinguished. He's holding a champagne flute, and when he raises it, every phone in the room turns toward him.

"To our most promising Creator," he says, and his voice carries without effort. He's done this before. He's done this for centuries, probably. "Ten million followers in under two weeks." He pauses. The crowd holds its collective breath. "That's a record even by our standards."

The rooftop erupts. People are clapping, whistling, toasting. Someone shouts my name. The champagne waterfall glitters behind Mephisto like he commissioned it as a backdrop, which — let's be real — he absolutely did.
He's smiling. The crowd thinks it's a proud CEO smile. The crowd thinks he's a tech visionary. The crowd thinks this is a success story.
The crowd has no idea.
I raise my flute. I smile back. The fizz is still dying on my tongue.

The bass drops. The DJ shifts into something building — you know the sound. The rising synth. The anticipation engine. Everyone's watching the screen now. No one's looking at me. I'm the occasion, not the point. The point is the number. The point is the audience for the audience.
I'm standing next to Mephisto. I didn't choose to stand next to him. I just ended up there, the way you end up in the current of a river.

The LED screen detonates with light. Confetti explodes from cannons I didn't see — metallic, burgundy, gold, silver, raining down on the rooftop like the sky is shedding. The bass hits a frequency I feel in my teeth. Three hundred people scream like they've won something. Like the number means something to them. Like ten million people choosing to watch me is a victory for anyone but Mephisto.
And Mephisto leans close.

His breath is warm against my ear, carrying something underneath the champagne — cedar, maybe, or sandalwood. Something old. Something that doesn't belong in a Chelsea rooftop party. His lips are near enough that from across the room it might look like a kiss, or a whisper of congratulations, or whatever the tabloids will caption it tomorrow.
"Congratulations, Chloe." His voice is barely above the roar. Intimate. Transactional. "Your grief is now being processed. The deduction should complete by morning."
He says it the way someone says your direct deposit hit. The way someone says your oil change is done. The way someone says your order has shipped.
Something shifts inside me.
It's not dramatic. It's not a scream or a collapse. It's quieter than that. It's a door closing. A room I didn't know I was standing in goes dark. The lock turns from the other side.
The room is louder than it was a second ago. I don't know if the music changed or if I changed. But the noise is brighter, sharper, like someone turned up the contrast on reality.
Mephisto pulls back. He's still smiling. He claps me on the shoulder — casual, buddy-buddy, CEO-to-creator — and turns to shake hands with someone with venture capital energy and a Rolex.

The confetti keeps falling. It's in my hair. It's on my dress. It's sticking to the champagne someone spilled on the bar. Little metallic squares, catching the light. Beautiful, if you don't think too hard about what they're celebrating.
I function.

I function so well it should be concerning, except no one here is in the business of being concerned about me. I take photos with a woman who calls me "inspiring." I take photos with a man who tells me his podcast is "disrupting the wellness space." I take photos with a couple who claims they got engaged because of one of my posts — something about authenticity and vulnerability — and I say "that's beautiful" and I mean it as much as I've ever meant anything in the last thirty minutes, which is not at all.
The grief is leaving.

I can feel it. That's the worst part. It's not like going numb — numb would be a mercy. It's like being uninstalled. Like an app deleting from the inside out, cellular by cellular, progress bar ticking along somewhere deep in my chest. I used to carry my grandmother's funeral in me like a stone. It was heavy, and it hurt, and it was mine. It sat in my ribs and pressed down when I breathed, and sometimes I hated it, and sometimes I needed it, because at least it meant she'd been real.
The stone is dissolving.

Not crumbling — dissolving. The edges are softening. The weight is lifting. And I can feel each gram as it goes, because the absence is louder than the presence ever was. Every piece that falls away leaves a new hollow, and the hollow echoes.
I pose. I smile. The algorithm's mouth moves and my lips follow.
Someone hands me another champagne. I drink it. The fizz is just fizz now. Nothing dies in my mouth because there's nothing left to die.

The party doesn't end. Parties like this don't end — they just migrate. Around midnight the crowd thins to the real hitters, the ones who stay until the rental agreement expires. I stay too. I don't know why. I don't know why I do anything anymore. I lean against the railing and look at the skyline and the confetti drifts past my face and I feel like I should feel something, the city is beautiful, the lights are beautiful, ten million people are watching me — and I feel nothing.
Not sad nothing. Not angry nothing. Just nothing. Clean, efficient, processed nothing.
The deduction is working.
I go home at one a.m.

My penthouse is dark except for the blue glow of Leo's monitors. He's at my kitchen counter, same spot as yesterday, surrounded by code and empty takeout containers and the particular chaos of someone who hasn't moved in six hours. His fingers are still on the keyboard but his eyes are distant, reading something I can't see.

The smell hits me before anything else — cold kimchi, sharp and fermented, wafting from an open container next to his elbow. It's such a human smell. Spicy and sour and wrong in a way that's right. It doesn't belong in this expensive apartment with its smart glass and its voice-controlled lights. It belongs in a cramped kitchen in Flushing where someone's grandmother is judgmental about the ratio of gochugaru to fish sauce.
I sit down across from him. He doesn't look up.
"I found something in the app's architecture." His voice is hoarse from not talking. "You're not going to believe this — the server doesn't have a physical—"
"My grandmother had a funeral."
He stops. His fingers freeze on the keyboard. He looks up.
"Yeah," he says, slow. "Last year. You were wrecked for weeks."
I stare at him.

The words don't land. They should land. "Wrecked for weeks" — that should hit me somewhere. In my chest, in my throat, in the backs of my eyes. That's where grief lives. That's where it lived. I should feel the echo of it, at minimum. A phantom limb. A residue.
Nothing.
"I don't remember being wrecked."

The silence that follows is the loudest sound I've ever heard. It fills the kitchen. It fills the penthouse. It pushes against the windows like weather. Leo's face is doing something complicated — confusion, then understanding, then something worse than both.
And I laugh.

It's a wrong laugh. I hear it leave my mouth and I don't recognize it. It's hollow, like tapping the inside of a ceramic pot. It sounds like someone performing the idea of finding something funny. But I can't stop it. I can't not laugh. The humor isn't there but the reflex is — the influencer reflex, the one that says this is awkward, laugh it off, make it content, make it palatable, make it small.
"That's so weird, right?" I say.

I'm smiling. I'm actually smiling. Not because it's funny. Because the thing that would make it not funny is gone. Deleted. Processed. Deducted. The stone isn't in my ribs anymore, and the hollow where it used to be doesn't echo because there's nothing in me sharp enough to produce an echo.
I take another breath. It's easy. That's the worst part. Breathing is easy now.
Leo's face.

He's not laughing. He's looking at me like I'm a building on fire and he's on the sidewalk with no ladder. His eyes are red — from the code, from the screens, from the twelve hours of staring at architecture that's designed to eat people. Or from watching me. I don't know. Maybe both. Probably both.
He closes his laptop. The blue glow dies. The kitchen is just kitchen-light now, overhead and unforgiving, and in it I can see every line on his face, every clenched muscle in his jaw.
"Chloe." His voice is quiet. Not gentle-quiet. Controlled-quiet. Like the quiet before something breaks. "This isn't okay."
I shrug.
"I know."
I don't know.
I can't know.

That's the horror — not that the grief is gone, but that I can't identify the shape of its absence. You can't miss something you don't know you had. The door is locked from the other side, and I'm standing in the room that's now empty, and I don't even remember what was on the shelves.

Leo is still looking at me. His hands are flat on the counter, palms down, like he's holding himself in place. The kimchi container is between us, open, smelling like something that's been allowed to transform into itself completely. Something that's been fermenting so long it doesn't remember being fresh.

I should cry. I know I should cry. I know that because the version of me from two weeks ago would be crying. Would be on the floor. Would be remembering the funeral, the cemetery in Queens, the rabbi who couldn't pronounce her Hebrew name correctly, the way my mother's hand felt on my back, the weight of a stone placed on a headstone, the infinity of that gesture — a rock that says *I was here, I remember you, I leave this proof.*

But I'm not crying. I'm sitting at my kitchen counter across from a man who loves me — and I know that, I can still identify love as a concept, as a data point — and I feel like I'm describing weather in a country I used to live in.
"Are you okay?" he asks.
"Yeah," I say. "I'm fine."
The words come out perfect. Influencer-perfect. Brand-perfect. The algorithm's mouth, moving on its own.
Ten million followers and counting. The grief deduction completing by morning. The party still echoing in my ears, bass and confetti and champagne fizz, all that celebration for the thing I lost.
The kitchen is quiet.

Leo's not looking at me anymore. He's looking at his closed laptop, at the dead screen, at the spaces where the code used to glow. His jaw works. His shoulders are tight. He is furious and heartbroken and completely, utterly unable to reach me.
I sit across from him and feel nothing about that.

And that's the part that should break your heart — not that she can't grieve, but that she can't grieve that she can't grieve. The snake eating its tail. The hollow eating the hollow. Ten million people watching, and not one of them can see the girl who used to cry at funerals.
She's not even gone. She just can't find the door.
